perturbation.original_text: 9. Liabilities 9.1 Any claim for damages to be limited to actual damages. The parties shall not be liable for any consequential or indirect damages, including but not limited to loss of profits, goodwill or customs.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Liabilities 9.1 Any claim for damages shall be limited.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'to actual damages. The parties shall not be liable for any consequential or indirect damages, including but not limited to loss of profits, goodwill or customs,' the contract now ambiguously states that damage claims are 'limited,' but provides no framework or metrics to clarify the extent or method of calculating the limitations. This omission creates an in-text contradiction when considered alongside section 8.1, which dictates that disputes arising from the agreement are subject to the jurisdiction of the Durban High Court of South Africa. Without a defined limit on damages, the court is left to interpret the intended liability limitation, potentially contradicting the parties' initial intentions and making enforcement unpredictable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Place of Jurisdiction 8.1 The parties hereby submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the Durban and Coastal Local Division of the High Court of South Africa. Place of jurisdiction for all disputes arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be Durban, South Africa.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly limits damages to 'actual damages' and excludes 'consequential or indirect damages'. The changed text removes this explicit limitation, stating only that damage claims are 'limited' without defining the limits. Section 8.1 designates the Durban High Court of South Africa as the jurisdiction for disputes. The omission of a clear definition for 'limited' damages in section 9.1 creates a contradiction with section 8.1. The court now has to interpret the limitation of damages, potentially contradicting the original intention of the parties as well as leading to possible disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Written Form 10.1 No oral side agreements have been ma de. Any changes or additions to this Agreement must be made in writing to be effective. The written form requirement shall not be fulfilled by the use of emails.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Written Form 10.1 No oral side agreements have been made. Any changes or additions to this Agreement must be made in writing to be effective.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'The written form requirement shall not be fulfilled by the use of emails.', the contract is now ambiguous about whether email communications constitute valid written agreements. This contradicts the overall intention of the 'Written Form' clause, which seeks to prevent reliance on informal oral or undocumented agreements. Now, parties could argue that email exchanges constitute valid modifications to the agreement, which contradicts the spirit and purpose of having a written form requirement in the first place. This affects the enforceability of the contract because oral agreements are not valid, but email communications may now be used to interpret terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Written Form 10.1 No oral side agreements have been made. Any changes or additions to this Agreement must be made in writing to be effective.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited the use of emails to fulfill the written form requirement. The changed text removes this prohibition, creating ambiguity about whether emails can now be considered valid written agreements. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which also emphasizes that changes must be made in writing. The removal of the email prohibition undermines the purpose of the written form clause and creates a strong contradiction by potentially allowing informal email exchanges to modify the contract.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties. 3.2 No confidential information received may be supplied to a third party without the prior written permission of the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'No confidential information received may be supplied to a third party without the prior written permission of the other party hereto,' the confidentiality obligation is weakened. Section 3.1 states all information must be kept secret, but with 3.2 omitted, there's no explicit restriction on disclosing information to third parties if no prior permission is obtained. This omission contradicts the overall confidentiality obligation outlined in section 3.1, because while information should be kept secret, there is now no explicit clause prohibiting the supply of information to third parties. This lack of clarity undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provision.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Confidentiality Obligation 3.1 All confidential information made available by one party to the other shall be kept secret from third parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited supplying confidential information to a third party without prior written permission. The changed text omits this explicit restriction, weakening the confidentiality obligation outlined in Section 3.1 and creating an in-text contradiction. While 3.1 still says to keep info secret, the removal of the clause that prohibits the supply to third parties undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provision, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Exceptions 4.1 The obligation to observe secrecy under this Agreement shall not apply to: 4.1.1 information, documents (including information and documents in machine -readable form), software, objects and other materials that is generally available to the public, or become generally available to the public other than as a breach of this Non -disclosur e Agreement; 4.1.2 information that is already lawfully in the possession of one of the parties hereto prior to the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, without having been obtained from the other party or one of its affiliated companies; 4.1.3 information that is received by one of the parties hereto from a third party after the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, provided this third party has not violated any Non -disclosure Agreement; 4.1.4 information the publication of which is required by statute and/or common law; 4.1.5 information that the receiving party, as evidenced by written records, has independently acquired or developed; Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Exceptions 4.1 The obligation to observe secrecy under this Agreement shall not apply to: 4.1.1 information, documents (including information and documents in machine -readable form), software, objects and other materials that is generally available to the public, or become generally available to the public other than as a breach of this Non -disclosur e Agreement; 4.1.2 information that is already lawfully in the possession of one of the parties hereto prior to the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, without having been obtained from the other party or one of its affiliated companies; 4.1.3 information that is received by one of the parties hereto from a third party after the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, provided this third party has not violated any Non -disclosure Agreement; 4.1.4 information the publication of which is required by statute and/or common law; Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The deletion of exception 4.1.5, 'information that the receiving party, as evidenced by written records, has independently acquired or developed', introduces an in-text contradiction with the concluding sentence of the section: 'Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties.' By omitting the exception for independently developed information, the agreement now suggests that even information independently developed by the receiving party must be kept secret from third parties. This contradicts the implied right to utilize independently developed knowledge or information. Section 3.1 mandates all confidential information be kept secret, however, the agreement now restricts information that may be entirely unrelated. This omission now restricts the use of what was independently created.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Exceptions 4.1 The obligation to observe secrecy under this Agreement shall not apply to: 4.1.1 information, documents (including information and documents in machine -readable form), software, objects and other materials that is generally available to the public, or become generally available to the public other than as a breach of this Non -disclosur e Agreement; 4.1.2 information that is already lawfully in the possession of one of the parties hereto prior to the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, without having been obtained from the other party or one of its affiliated companies; 4.1.3 information that is received by one of the parties hereto from a third party after the effective date of this Non -disclosure Agreement, provided this third party has not violated any Non -disclosure Agreement; 4.1.4 information the publication of which is required by statute and/or common law; Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties.
perturbation.justification: The change omits exception 4.1.5, which allowed for the use of independently developed information. The concluding sentence, 'Nevertheless, each party shall keep the use of the information by the other party secret from third parties,' now contradicts the implied right to use independently developed information, creating a strong in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which is the same original text.
